html:
Quarterhill (QTRH.TO): A Broken WiLAN Label Becoming a Scalable ITS Software Platform

Quarterhill is not the royalty-stream story in the prior draft. That was wrong. WiLAN was sold in 2023, with Quarterhill retaining only a 10% stake valued at US$3.9 million in Q1 2026 filings. The real thesis is cleaner: a former patent/licensing roll-up and troubled transportation integrator is turning into a focused intelligent transportation systems platform with improving gross margins, recurring service backlog, AI-enabled tolling/enforcement software, and a now-solvable 2026 debt wall.

The market still sees a small Canadian tech orphan with negative EPS, weak historical free cash flow, and a convertible maturity. The variant view is that Q1 2026 was the first clean evidence that the restructuring is working. Quarterhill reported revenue up 14% to US$38.6 million, gross margin up to 28% from 12%, and its third straight positive adjusted EBITDA quarter, while backlog reached US$428.8 million, or more than 2.5x trailing revenue. The company also secured a US$60 million credit facility plus a US$100 million accordion, with the delayed draw expected to fund repayment of the October 2026 convert. That removes the most obvious bear overhang without equity dilution.

The forward economics are the point. At the latest TSX close of C$1.80 on May 15, 2026, because TSX is closed May 18 for Victoria Day, Quarterhill is valued at about C$216 million of equity and roughly US$195 million enterprise value after pro forma net debt. That is not optically cheap on trailing losses, but trailing numbers are stale. If management gets anywhere near its stated goal of double-digit EBITDA in 2026 and an exit-2027 EBITDA margin approaching 20%, the stock is being valued at roughly 5x to 7x normalized 2028 EBITDA for a government-contract, mission-critical infrastructure software and enforcement platform.

The key change is repeatability. Management says the new tolling platform lets them bid more like a technology company and less like a custom systems integrator. That matters because old Quarterhill won complex projects that could consume margin; new Quarterhill wants to reuse a base platform across agencies, price competitively, and still expand margin. Recent awards support the transition: a US$6.3 million new state agency tolling platform win, US$13 million of Indiana DOT WIM/VWIM awards, US$2.4 million of Caltrans projects, a New Jersey statewide traffic-monitoring contract, a US$6.4 million MassDOT WIM expansion, and a US$10.7 million Illinois Tollway extension.

Retail attention is still early. Reddit chatter has been mostly “bottom feeding” and breakout-focused, not a developed fundamental thesis, while StockTwits shows essentially no meaningful sentiment or message volume. Analyst coverage is thin too, with ATB Cormark’s visible target raised to C$2.75 in April. That is exactly the setup: a small, ignored, historically messy company where the screen still shows losses but the next twelve months can show a very different margin profile.

Risks are real. This is still a project business with fixed-price contract risk, working-capital volatility, customer concentration in government agencies, and debt that becomes more traditional but not trivial. Q1 operating cash flow was negative US$5.4 million, and stock-based compensation was high relative to EBITDA. The bull case needs Q2 through Q4 to prove that 28% gross margin is not a one-quarter artifact and that the new platform does not become another customized services burden. M&A is also double-edged: buying tolling contracts at 2x to 4x EBITDA could be highly accretive, but the new accordion can also tempt management into empire building.

Still, the setup is attractive because the balance of evidence has changed before the valuation has fully changed. The convert overhang is no longer existential. Backlog is large. Q1, normally the softest quarter, was EBITDA-positive. The company is winning in marquee transportation markets. If revenue grows into the fixed cost base and gross margin holds in the high-20s or moves into the 30s, the earnings power can move faster than the share price already has.

Bear case: C$1.00, down 44%. Gross margin falls back, EBITDA stays low single digits, cash flow remains negative, and investors value Quarterhill as a levered project integrator.

Base case: C$3.15, up 75%. Revenue reaches roughly US$190 million to US$205 million, EBITDA approaches a US$35 million run-rate by late 2027, the convert is repaid without dilution, and the stock trades around 8.5x forward EBITDA.

Bull case: C$4.75, up 164%. The platform model works, EBITDA margins approach 20%, backlog converts cleanly, and disciplined M&A adds incremental EBITDA at attractive multiples, letting the market re-rate Quarterhill as a scarce small-cap ITS software and enforcement platform rather than a failed legacy roll-up.
